mnemonics.title: Matrix Inverse Definition
mnemonics.content: AA⁻¹ = A⁻¹A = I (Identity) - Think: 'A times A-inverse gives Identity, nothing more to prove'

acronyms.expansion: Augment with Identity, Reduce to Identity

acronyms.usage: Used to compute adjugate: adj(A) = transpose of cofactor matrix

visualTips.visualization: Swap diagonal elements, negate off-diagonals, divide by determinant. Remember: [[a,b],[c,d]] → [[d,-b],[-c,a]]/det
Imagine the augmented matrix [A|I] as two halves. As left half transforms to identity (like melting ice to water), right half transforms to inverse (mirrored transformation).
Think of singular as 'single' or 'one' - determinant = 1, but singular matrices have determinant = 0 (zip, nothing)

analogies.analogy: Like reciprocal in scalar math: just as 5 × (1/5) = 1, matrix A × A⁻¹ = I. Division doesn't exist for matrices; inverse is its replacement.
Invertible matrix is like a reversible process (you can undo it). Singular is like a one-way street (you can't go back - information is lost).
